item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is awful object. Yes, it has got a good history, but it cannot save all topic.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes it is nice and brief and if you squint alludes to something significant. A few points:

1. The science needs tidying up, I know that the foundation deals with things that defy science but the terms used and the way they are used are actually nonsensical.

2. Use of the word "notably" seems off… something about saying it is notable and then not saying why.

Here is my suggestion:

//Item #: SCP-3491

Object Class: Euclid

Special Containment Procedures: SCP-3491-1 is suspended from the ceiling of a 5m x 5m x 5m storage room within Site-15 such that SCP-3491 is suspended in the centre of the room. All surfaces within the room are lined with aluminium and are directly connected to the grounding system of Site-15. Level-2 whole-body electrical insulation equipment must be worn before entering SCP-3491's containment room. All subject which come within 1 meter of SCP-3491 should immediately withdraw and submit themselves for medical assessment.

Description: SCP-3491 is a paper origami bird suspended in the center of a bird cage of unknown material (SCP-3491-1). The left wing of SCP-3491 sustained damage prior to it's acquisition by the foundation (a small rip) the circumstances leading to this damage are currently unknown.

Whenever a human subject enters a 10 meter radius of SCP-3491 it begins to emit a beam of beta radiation towards the subject. The beam cross section is such that the maximum intensity is directed to the centre of mass of the subject and radiation monitoring equipment within the room has confirmed that 100% of the radiation is absorbed by the subject.

The intensity and energy of the beam both increase exponentially as the distance between the subject and SCP-3491. Within a 1 meter radius the rate of charge build up presents a risk of electrical burns, within 5 cm the radiation is sufficiently intense that all known materials disintegrate.

Attempts to remove SCP-3491 from SCP-3491-1 without direct contact have resulted in sudden increases in beam energy and intensity inevitably resulting in the death of the person attempting to manipulate SCP-3491. An attempt at remote robotic manipulation resulted in [data redacted].

Material recovered from SCP-3491-1 suggest it is composed of non-anomalous steel; it is currently unknown how it is able to avoid damage from the emissions of SCP-3941//

Not a perfect representation of science but better and it alludes to the previous damage in a more clinical and analytic way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree it's nice to have a short interesting little SCP that doesn't go on for an hour ,I like long SCP's but shorter SCP's are easier to understand ,and you get them quicker ,and if done right they can be some really great ideas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand why people like this, but for me, it's not exactly a "story" that's being told; it's a basic cause-and-effect example that's seen every day in almost every living thing. That is: entity becomes hurt -> entity becomes defensive. While I appreciate that this is told mostly subtly, I just don't think it offers much.

I did say it was mostly subtle; I think the last line is incredibly "in your face". I think it's rather obvious that no further damage would be taken by the object if it incinerated everything that comes close to it. I also don't think it's supposed to tell us that the object is unaffected by its own electric shock. I think that line is the author TELLING us the story. The realization that people are praising here is that "this SCP, built by human hands, took damage, and developed this anomaly in order to prevent further damage." The selection of detail in the final line very obviously points to this, and acts as a "Hey, this is what this story is really about". I think the article could do just fine without it, and would help it a lot. In its current state, the last line seems to doubt the intelligence of its readers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. I've been biased against short articles for a while now, primarily because I was under the impression they were left behind in Series 1 after reading some of the more well-loved Series 4 articles, but this proved me wrong. Very well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At leas you can measure the impact of this by how many comments and votes (up and down) it got in such a short time. I saw articles that took years to reach the comment page this thing got in less than a month.

Now, I'll upvote because I want it to linger. I like it enough and I see a niche for it in the site. But it is far from as good as some people are saying, and certainly there is still room for improvement, even in this limited concept (limited on scope, not value).

My first problem is, actually, not with it being too short, but being too long. There is still some fat to be trimmed. The second paragraph of containment can be thrown away, for an example. Decontamination of electrons is too simple a task to be referred that way, you have just to ground yourself.

The second and third paragraphs of the description could be shortened to:

Starting at 10 meters from SCP-3491, human subjects start to accumulate negative charge. This charge increases with proximity until reaching dangerous levels. Within 5 centimeters it is enough to fatally discharge through any protective material.

By the way, the complete dissolution of matter is a bit too much for me. The last paragraph could read

Those effects do not seem to cause any damage to SCP-3491.

Otherwise, it is good, not overwhelming. The concept is too simple, its only real good effect is being short. Good enough, I guess, but the space that is saved could be used for improving it. A comment, a research note, anything could help. But given the risk of wasting the concept, just trimming fat could be enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just to say that yes, positrons are antimatter electrons. It could cause much more harm if it created positrons on you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ah, i remember when this was one of the lowest-rated articles…

this makes me think of Series I, but in the best possible way. it's not trying to tell some complex story about itself or the Foundation, it just…exists. even then, it goes a bit beyond just "thing that does a thing," since there is a slight story attached to it. it's simple, it's subtle, and it makes me feel nostalgic. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's good, simple, anomalous, and interesting.

People applauding it as genius are being pretty hyperbolic, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm surprised how much praise something so cliche is getting. The angle of "a paper bird that was betrayed once and now nothing can ever ever touch it ever" just isn't compelling to me and hardly hit me with a "whooaaa" moment; it wasn't this huge revelation for me that other people had. I don't understand it myself, but I guess it's a matter of preference. Gonna downvote this one because I didn't enjoy it at all, but I recognize that other people disagree with this opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seems to be grammatically and tonally…terrible? I kind of am not getting the praise it's getting.

Reading the comments, there is a quiet beauty to it. But is nobody else missing a more clinical tone to this? It kind of sticks out. No-voting for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this, I do, but I'm having difficulty accepting the story. So, in the past, someone mishandled this origami bird, and its wing ripped. Because of this mishandling (in the same way an abused dog may become aggressive later on), the bird now delivers a life-threatening shock to anyone that comes nearby.

Now that's a fine interpretation of the story the article might be trying to tell, but it's not compelling enough for me to enjoy. And it gives me more questions than answers: how much was the bird mishandled before it was ripped? It is made of paper, so that couldn't have been much, and so you have to ask if the way it reacted is proportional to the amount it was mishandled. And I'm not about to assess the emotional state of an origami bird because there's already enough of an assumption made before you get to this point.

Further, how did the Foundation get it into the cage if it was shocking people? Did they discover it in the cage? If they did, how were they able to transport it if it was shocking people and dissolving matter? Perhaps it developed its shocking anomaly after being contained by the Foundation: it was abused by one of its captors and it fears that its new captors will do the same thing.

I will say I enjoy a minimal story, but in this case I feel as though it's almost too brief, and so you have to make up the story yourself, really. So I think I'm caught between a novote and a downvote.

item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Images used in posted articles must comply with the site's image policy. A source must be provided on the discussion page and the source must be compatible with the site's license. The image has thus been removed until a compliant source for the image can be found. The author has been notified by PM.

I'm aware there's a (non-CC) source above. This means the author will need permission to use it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's apparently brought in from URL https://jlroeder.files.wordpress.com/2016/07/birdcage.jpg?w=840, which is some guy's blog, apparently. Unless the author received permission to use it (which I doubt), this isn't CC.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like minimalism, and I like articles that amount to little more than fairly engaging story-prompts.

I think this would have worked better if the mention of the slightly ripped wing was the final line, though. That would draw the reader's attention to it, and make the 'a-ha!' moment significantly more solid. It makes it feel more important, which also gives it a sense of emotional pathos.

Something like this:

Attempts to remove SCP-3491-1 have been unsuccessful. Within five centimeters, the charge generated between an approaching object and SCP-3491-1 becomes strong enough to result in the complete dissolution of matter. This renders any protective material worn by subjects useless. No components of SCP-3491 have sustained any damage following the generation of these intense electric charges.

Notably, one of SCP-3491's wing tips is slightly ripped.

ETA: Is the author (or anyone else, for that matter) going to cite the source for the image?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, this one took a little while for me. I didn't like it initially, but in some ways it kinda feels like a return to some of the better moments of SI - it's simple, but leaves plenty of room for imagination when it comes to completing a "narrative." +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not surprised that its characterization can come across as flat… given that it's made of paper :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah. Well. It's pretty shallow, innit?

The emotions that come with someone close to you hurting you can be really complicated, and I'd need to see more of that complexity reflected in an article before I would get on board.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I honestly don't think the article is as marvelous as others are making it to be, and even after the "ooohhhh" moment i'd otherwise stay at a nonvote, but I'm gonna upvote this just because of how efficient this is.

It's short, but it has everything it needs. It's a "thing that does thing", but it has a little story behind it. It seems boring at the start, but there's a little twist you might miss.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ta da

It's not amazing, but it's not a "thing that does thing" as you'd first assume.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd go with "succorance"
This reminds me of everything that made series I spectacular.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Somehow that makes this…lesser, to me.

I'm not sure why, I just didn't expect it to be so pedestrian.

I saw that, and made the connection and figured I was being too obvious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone tore it and then it was able to zap things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. I thought it was really obvious, and I tend to be dense as a white dwarf when it comes to these things. Motivation aside, it should be clear at least that it was folded and ripped before gaining zappy powers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a hell of a logic leap/assumption.

Personally, I'm not buying that, it doesn't really track.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ You sure you want this?
- An inquisitive one. Intriguing.

It's wing is ripped, but nothing can touch it. How?

Conclusion: it trusted something, or someone. It was betrayed, and it never wants anything or anyone ever again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Notably, one of SCP-3491's wing tips is slightly ripped.

Within five centimeters, the charge generated between an approaching object and SCP-3491-1 becomes strong enough to result in the complete dissolution of matter. This renders any protective material worn by subjects useless.

Think about it. How?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm having the same reaction.

Multiple people have said there's some deeper meaning, but I can't see it even after a few read throughs.

Maybe I'm just dense, someone drop the "story" in a collapsible for me, I guess. Novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is pretty goddamn refreshing.

Noticing this still has no title, might I suggest to the author (if they come across this): "A Scar"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As others have said, this looks very much like 'a thing that does a thing' on first glance, but I didn't find it much more interesting after further consideration. While there is certainly a subtle, hidden story here, I just don't think it's a particularly compelling one; I can't surmise more from the article other than that the bird was damaged in some way and is now afraid, which isn't enough for me to have a reaction to.

There may be something deeper I'm missing, but too much of it is left up to the reader's imagination, and I'm not convinced anything else I can add to it is related to the article as opposed to my own wild speculation. I can see some people like this sort of minimalist approach, and it's aesthetically pleasing in a way, but it's more like something you would see in a piece of visual art, and I'm not really a fan of this in writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I downvoted right away.

It's only after going through these comments, sure that I missed something, that I found I had indeed missed something. This isn't any old object with any old effect tacked on, it's much more than that. I immediately corrected to an upvote.

I fear that too many had my initial response, but not my second one. It's a shame because this is a great example of how things should be

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's short, and it progresses. It could be more, but it is sufficient as it is. I'd recommend going through and making sure all the terms in this piece are used in the order that they are explained in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only critique I have of this is as follows: You refer to SCP-3491 and then all of a sudden, it's SCP-3491-1 with no SCP-3491-2 or anything of the sort. If you change all of these to just SCP-3491, then I'll have no complaints! I liked the article a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, upvoting because this is a little bit brilliant. I agree you should clean it up, but I'll be damned if it isn't a great example of short-form done well. I hope people might take a little more care reading it now it's got some positive responses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did not notice this upon a first read. The "how did its wing get ripped" type thing. After that realization, this hit home hard. I got emotional shivers from this thing. This is brilliant, and if it goes down, it really needs to get like, archived in some way.

EDIT: Is this ever going to get a mainlist title? If the author just goes defunct and never gets to it, how long until someone just up and names the thing? Could someone at least put in a placeholder?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
negatively charged electrons

As opposed to positive ones (yes, I've heard of positrons, but those are technically antimatter electrons)?

Anyway, lord knows I'm a tremendous sucker for brevity and things left implied rather than said. This is like, the condensed Platonic ideal of a skip. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I at first no voted because I appreciated the angle taken, but thought that it wasn't very clear. Although I realized that I figured out what the cool part of it was while I was reading, and then somehow promptly forgot this detail when I switched back over to chat.

At the end of the article, I immediately asked myself, "well how did it rip its wing if nothing could get close?" Which as that sinks in a bit, I realize that its just enough for me to realize the story if I had just given it a bit more care.

Take my +1. The brevity of this is quite impressive, and while the story is simple, its more than enough to justify an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy this, and that's a statement I'm finding myself making less and less these days.

Just because something is simple does not mean that it is bad, nor does it mean that there isn't a story being told, just because it's not spelled out with prose or slapped across the reader's face.

It might look like a "Thing What Does a Thing," but it does that well: it's not ridiculously over-the-top, but neither does it just sit there existing and performing some task. The bird, apparently, doesn't want to be touched; if it wants, is aware of itself, then it's some form of "alive." That alone poses enough of a question for me: what made this bird, literally made by the touch of a person, completely reject all further contact? It's damaged, it's been hurt, and it doesn't want to be hurt again.

djkaktus called it a "biting dog" in chat, and that's as good an analogy as any I'd make. Author, please don't be discouraged by the (possible) poor performance of this, and keep at it. I think I'd like to see more from you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of love this. If I might recommend a few minor alterations.

You can further simplify your containment procedures with just the following:

SCP-3491 is currently held at Site-15. No subject may come within a meter of SCP-3491 for more than 30 seconds unless proper equipment is provided.

That's all. Then, getting into the rest of this:

SCP-3491 is a paper origami bird within a steel bird cage. SCP-3491-1 is suspended in the center of the cage by unknown means. Notably, one of SCP-3491's wing tips is slightly ripped.

See?

Beginning at a distance of 10 meters from the entity, negatively charged electrons can be detected increasing in energy between SCP-3491 and a human subject. This charge increases exponentially as the subject moves closer, until becoming dangerous to the subject. Overexposure to the charge produced by SCP-3491 can result in a concentrated burn at the vertical center of the individual.

Attempts to remove SCP-3491-1 have been unsuccessful. Within five centimeters, the charge generated between an approaching object and SCP-3491-1 becomes strong enough to result in the complete dissolution of matter. This renders any protective material worn by subjects useless.

No components of SCP-3491 have sustained any damage following the generation of these intense electric charges.

Just come cleaning up.

Really kind of lovely.
